fresh_facts.claim: President Trump announced a conditional two-week ceasefire with Iran on April 7, 2026, suspending U.S. bombing in exchange for Iran halting defensive operations and ensuring safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz.
The Pentagon declared victory over Iran under Operation Epic Fury, detailed in a briefing by Secretary of War Pete Hegseth and Joint Chiefs Chairman Gen. Dan Caine on April 8, 2026.
Explosions were reported on Iranian Gulf islands including near Lavan Island Oil Refinery (at least three blasts) and Siri Island hours after the ceasefire announcement on April 9, 2026.
The IRGC stated it will respect the ceasefire per orders from Supreme Leader Ayatollah Seyyed Mojtaba Khamenei but will keep 'fingers on the trigger' and remain prepared for escalated response.
Iran launched missiles intercepted by Jordan and a missile barrage targeted by UAE air defenses; Kuwait responded to an extensive wave of Iranian drones.
A gas processing facility in Abu Dhabi was hit by Iranian fire, igniting a fire that restarted after temporary halt on April 9, 2026.
Iranian Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi confirmed halting defensive operations if the U.S. stops attacks and proposed a 10-point plan including U.S. withdrawal and Strait control.
Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was killed in late February 2026 in U.S./Israel strikes; his son Mojtaba Khamenei was appointed successor by the Assembly of Experts.
President Masoud Pezeshkian faces complete political deadlock and backlash for signaling conditional war-ending willingness, with the IRGC assuming key state functions.
Pezeshkian appointed IRGC hardliner Mohammad Bagher Zolghadr as Supreme National Security Council secretary, replacing assassinated Ali Larijani.
Tensions exist between IRGC chief Ahmad Vahidi and President Pezeshkian over war management and economic fallout.
Hardliners branded ex-Foreign Minister Mohammad Javad Zarif a traitor for anti-war calls and urged his arrest along with ex-President Hassan Rouhani.
State TV warned civilians against revealing officials' locations and frames protests as enemy warfare.
President Trump announced productive and constructive talks with Iran, postponing strikes on its power grid and energy infrastructure for at least five days.
U.S. envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are leading negotiations with a top Iranian official according to Trump's claim.
Iran denies direct talks with the U.S. but acknowledges messages via friendly states including Egypt, Pakistan, and Turkey.
A 15-point U.S. peace plan delivered via Pakistan calls for reopening the Strait of Hormuz, missile limits, sanctions relief, and nuclear rollback.
Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi is reviewing the U.S. peace plan without intent to negotiate, while a senior official insists war ends only on Tehran's terms.
Trump issued ultimatums to reopen the Strait by Tuesday or face destruction of power plants and threatened seizing Kharg Island and Iran's oil.
Iran released 20 cargo ships starting Monday but portrays Strait control as a financial asset.
War exacerbates sanctions' toll on livelihoods, with IRGC-Pezeshkian disputes over economic damage.
Trump ratcheted sanctions prior to the war, and threats target infrastructure and oil to force Strait reopening.
State broadcaster escalates anti-dissent rhetoric, portraying protests as enemy-orchestrated amid strikes on intelligence compounds and Basij bases.
Recent protests are cited as the most serious unrest ever faced by the regime.
Israel accelerated strikes on Tehran airports, aircraft, and IRGC intelligence chief Majid Khademi.
Houthis entered the war, potentially threatening Bab al-Mandab.
The U.S. rescued a downed F-15E pilot.
Iran's parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf denied U.S. talks on social media.

regime_fall.current_status: Day 43 crisis intensifies with contradictory signals: Pentagon declares victory while Iranian forces continue strikes on Gulf infrastructure and regional targets despite ceasefire announcement, revealing severe regime fracture between hardliners and reformists, compounded by Trump's ultimatums on Strait reopening and threats to Iran's energy infrastructure within five days.
regime_fall.last_major_event: Trump issued ultimatum to reopen Strait of Hormuz by Tuesday or face destruction of power plants and seizure of Kharg Island, while Iran's hardliners and reformists clash over ceasefire acceptance and Foreign Minister Araghchi reviews but rejects negotiation of a 15-point U.S. peace plan on April 9, 2026.
